commentBody: “All the vowels are in order!” Tom said, facetiously.
Kayo 🙄🙄🙄

Again, I finished 33.7% faster than my average for Tuesdays.  Half a minute faster than my Monday average.

Yup, I suddenly must be getting much faster in my dotage.  That must be it.  That's the ticket...
Very convoluted for a Tuesday puzzle. When I read that the maker had done mostly Saturdays, it made more sense.
40 across is a unit of energy. A watt is a unit of work.

I have my reservations about 5A's (KAYO) clue, [Bout rout].

A knockout is certainly decisive, taking the bout out of the hands of the judges and their questionable scorecards, but knockouts don't have to be a rout.  Sure, Ali's first-round KO of Liston counts as such, but his KO of Wepner in the 15th round, after having been to the mat himself, was no rout.

I know, good enough for crosswords, and like the [Hot spot]/DESERT clue, there's some overlap, but a KO isn't necessarily a rout.

But the editors sure like those singsong clues!

While I love that "supervocalic" is in fact supervocalic, it bugs me that it's not the vowels in order. Maybe we should rename it to sapervicoluc ...

(I am even more bothered that "palindrome" isn't one, but palindromeemordnilap is too long and palinnilap hasn't caught on.)

A fine Tuesday, more or less themeless to solve. As a Cincinnati transplant, the first job when you get here is to learn how to spell Cincinnati. The second is to eat the chili. (I know, Mean Old Lady, I know).

"I can't think of any more supervocalic words!"
"I guess you'll have to throw in the vowel."

("O, sometimes I wonder Y I talk to U.")
Not just Y, but sometimes also W.

Excuse me, Mr. Blair, aren't you being a bit ABSTEMIOUS in not meaning that hallowed word?

A good enough Tuesday to keep me off the streets and the them GAVE IT FOCUS.  I've never heard anyone call it Apple ICloud, but I'm not a computer guy, so maybe there's like, Verizon ICloud or AOL ICloud. 

Nice little corners, such as Unwed/Undead, the jailhouse trio of Do it! Shiv, Use On made me think I'd better watch my back around here; shot crossing On the Wagon was wicked. Not the biggest fan of ires, oh dang, kayo, but in the end I'm okay with kayo at least. 

NATO.

I got hung up on the middle right more than usual for a Tuesday. I think USE ON was hard to get, and then I had no idea what a boilermaker is so that didn’t help. Still fun tho, I liked the AEIOU reveal.
TIL that GOOGLE DRIVE has the same number of letters as APPLE ICLOUD.
…KAYO?

Raising my hand because of course I had IRkS before IRES. Who wouldn’t?

BASE as “a safe place to stand on a diamond” confused me. A base IS a diamond. So how could it be ON the diamond? Which is why I didn’t fill this in until my last pass .

APPLE ICLOUD is how nobody refers to it, ever. 

I don’t know. Overall, this was a fine Tuesday puzzle. Some of it just felt a bit forced.

Props to Count CHOCULA, though. “I vant to eat your cereal!”
Next puzzle should just have IRIS over and over based on how often I have seen it lately.
Spelling out “KAYO”? Please. Weak sauce.

DCON/CHOCULA was absolutely a natick for me. Too UScentric. 

I don’t think I have used the expression to HASH anything OUT but that seemed as a reasonably old Americanism. 

The rest of the puzzle was a reasonable Tuesday.
What is wrong with the midi puzzle ? It says page no longer exits - then later on it shows up ???

The last clue I couldn’t get, despite having a family member with this disorder, was OCD. Because it’s not a habit. It’s a compulsion. I feel like this clue perpetuates a common misunderstanding about a very serious disorder.

This is a niche nit, but this comment crowd is full of niche nit nuts so i feel I’m in the right place to vent:

ETON is *not* a prep school! It might be what the Americans call a prep school but it’s a British school, and in Britain a “prep school” is a private primary school, while Eton is a private secondary school. So if you went to Eton and asked the pupils how they liked their prep school, they would tell you about place they studied before Eton. Because Eton is not a prep school. 

(In fact, in Britain a private secondary school is known as a “public school”, but that’s another level of complication… let’s not get started!)

Definitely a grid I'm thankful to solve on an app rather than ink.

I had the entire bottom right solved bar one entry before realising I had made several mistakes.

Quite rare, but also quite enjoyable - having a lot of something wrong is so much nicer than having a lot of nothing because I don't know.
Today I learned what a NEAP tide is, last answer for me just by guessing the crossing name GIA…Anyone else..??
Count Chocula hive rise

A fun theme that had me thinking of examples from recent headlines. 

[Deep space travelers]
ARTEMIS FOUR

[Flag restored to Stonewall National Monument]
WAVE IT PROUDLY

Well done, Mark.
I’m old and could use a dictionary of texting shortcuts.

I’m beginning to suspect that the NTT xword editors have been reading my calendar. On Saturday I reported that I had been doing the xword in the intervals of an adaptation of Olga T’s “Drive your Plows..”
Tomorrow I am seeing an adaptation of CLUE, albeit known here as Cluedo. 
I’m beginning to think that I am going to have to check my theatre programmes every time I attempt one of these xwords.

Although, in the puzzle, we've seen "ire" used a a verb, somehow my mind read it as that old stand-by of puzzle construction, the plural of a normally uncountable noun. Well, the OED cites only one instance of that, from the Wycliffte Bible of 1425 (where, coincidentally, it is spelled "iris"):
"He that stirith iris [Latin iras], bringith forth discordis."

Early morning--time to shower and shave, with that razor I borrowed from Occam.

So, finding words and phrases that use all the vowels just once, is hard enough, but it can be done.  I’m thinking of the GENIUS AT WORK in MOZAMBIQUE who AMBIDEXTROUSLY ate CAULIFLOWER, played the TAMBOURINE, and, while thinking of JULIA ROBERTS and the FOUNDING FATHERS, wrote with a FOUNTAIN PEN.

But, getting the vowels in words and phrases just once *in order* MAKES IT TOUGH, and I believe it would make for an involving LATE-NIGHT HOUR activity … okay I’m only saying this HALF SERIOUSLY, please don’t TAKE IT OUT on me!

My hand is down.  I took IRES to be a noun: “There were multiple ires among the disgruntled employees.”

Thanks for a fun solve.
Congrats on the cycle, Mark! A smooth -- too smooth? -- Tuesday, with a revealer I did not guess before reading the clue.
Very cheeky IONIAN/DOHA cross. The O was my last square.

It helps to read clues carefully. I kept reading 5A as “Boat route,” so when I finally figured out YEW for 7D, I was thoroughly confused. When I didn’t get the music, I went back through and discovered I had spelled NEAP wrong. Then I was irked (not IRED) that KAYO was really KO spelled out. Really? 17D 😞

Well, talk about range.  Mark’s last puzzle, in February, was an uber-low 62-word Saturday, with eight answers of 11 letters or more – all of them NYT debuts!  It was tough, too.

Today’s puzzle hit the other end of the difficulty continuum, but I made it tough by trying to guess the revealer (Hi, @Barry!).  I jumped through hoops  – looking at the first letters of the theme-answer words, looking at first words, last words, looking for hidden words – until I finally uncle-ed.

When I uncovered the revealer, I just shook my head in amazement over how something can be right in front of my eyes, and I just don’t see it.

What I did see as I solved were colorful theme answers (well, maybe not APPLE ICLOUD, but look at the others!).  APPLE crossing SEED made me think of Johnny, and FROZEN abutting WATER made me think of ice.

Mark, word-nerd me loved this theme, and congratulations on your 79th Times puzzle.  Please, keep the streak going, and thank you!
Today's New Yorker, by the NYT's own Wyna Liu, might be the chewiest puzzle I've done in a while: https://www.newyorker.com/puzzles-and-games-dept/crossword/2026/04/14

The Y in WHATSNEWWITHYOU is admittedly a consonant there, but (to me) it's just not a good look, as some might say. I guess I'm just expecting better execution from an experienced constructor in a NYT puzzle. Or maybe I'm just especially cranky today. Fill like IMED, IRES (which at least pairs with IRIS), DCON, AAHS (and AWW), INHD, and USEON didn't help.

Not a favorite, but seemed OK for a Tuesday.

For me. it was a breath of fresh air to see a puzzle by a constructor whose been published here for more than 40 years, as contradictory as it must sound. I just wish the puzzle, while not bad, had been a little more interesting.

Nice Tuesday puzzle and an enjoyable workout. Just a bit surprised that no one else has mentioned a Sunday puzzle from September 30, 2007 by Kelsey Blakley with the title "Five String." AEIOU was also the 'reveal' in that one. Some example answers: GAMEMISCONDUCT FACETIOUS HALFSERIOUSLY GATHERINGCLOUDS WANDERINGSOUL TAKEITOUT WATERINGTROUGH TRADEDISCOUNT LATENIGHTHOUR Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=9/30/2007&g=17&d=D ....
I know it has shown itself before, but KAYO really IRES me.

Another nice one. Not too much trivia, DCON was the hardest. I don't mind not knowing how the theme worked, or at least not trying to work it out. I've never thought of Y as a vowel, (and I taught children the vowels) I think it must be a US thing.

That felt quite chewy for a Tuesday over here. I started to panicked a tad when I had nothing on the top line, but MANX offered a starting point, phew.
I needed the revealer to get the theme, obvious when it’s pointed out.
I’ve muttered in a reply to @Petrol below. As they point out, ETON is in no way a Prep school. I understand @Steve L’s point that it would be considered a prep in American terms, but I would say that if a clue is based on something as specifically British as that school, then the British term of Public school is a more appropriate fit. But, tomayto/tomahto. We won’t fall out about it.

Am I to understand that CHOCULA is a reference to a children’s breakfast cereal? Sounds tooth cloyingly sweet.

I imagine that there's a computer program you can set up that will furnish you with AEIOU-in-order theme answers.  Otherwise, having to dream up these answers would be A tEdIOUs process, indeed.

The theme was irrelevant to my solve, but might have been more helpful had it been a much harder puzzle.  At any rate, any theme is fine with me if it leads the way to long colorful theme answers like these on a Tuesday.  A clean junk-free grid and a nice puzzle.
A fine Tuesday puzzle!

Shouted AIH when I realized for the NTH time that there is no such thing as a MINX cat… 

Also, I’m happy to see SHIV crossing over from my other latest obsession (Slay The Spire 2)
Here is a Thursday from the archive I ran across yesterday that owes something to today's puzzle (or vice versa). https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/daily/2018/11/15

Such a diehl!  Mark!  Where ya been?

Is W your fave letter?  After 16A and 51D, I had to wonder...  
Was this one of those puzzles with every letter in the alphabet?  I don't recall seeing a Q....
OH, DANG?  That's it?  Come on, already!

I would be more praiseful, except I am IRKed that you used IRES to satisfy the clue at 32A (Angers.)

As usual on a Tuesday, I solved from the top down, and tried to guess the theme as I was solving.  My first guess was that it was going to be different pronunciations of the OU-diphthong, but that hypothesis was falsified with OUT and CLOUD.

By the time I got to the bottom, I had no trouble identifying the quintet in question, and I was able to go back and enjoy an "A-ha! moment".  
It did help with my last themer:  having filled in TROUGH already, it confirmed by suspicion that the "ranch fixture" was going to involve WATERING [A-E-I].

So?
My assessment?  
Nice Tuesday puzzle.
Thanks, Mark Diehl!

Side Question for the linguists among us:  Do you pronounce the -ph- of diphthong as a F-sound or a P-sound?  I've always pronounced it as a P, and an F sounds very wrong to my ear, but it seems that the F-pronunciation is preferred.

Maybe I should take the day off?  I spent five minutes wondering what kind of benefits package a Hen gets at the local co-op before my brain recognized that it inserted a dash where it shouldnt be. My brain is officially out of office. (This was in the Midi) Haha!
A fun Tuesday run-about but I can’t say I understood the theme until I read the tell-all. Now that I know, I know, you know?

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ stand
stand a while
through the angers
stand
for the defeats
stand still
stand still
d/ one
just one
the belief of one
the practice of one
the one in the crowd

a/ you

SWALLWELL IS OUT

Question:  In Ancient Greece, IONIA referred to the Greek settlements/colonies on the Western shores of Asia Minor (Anatolia, Turkey), and IONIc referred to the dialect of Greek spoken there.

When did the IONIAN Sea move to the waters between Greece and Italy?

I thought IRE could be used as a verb. (Am I too old?) And KAYO must be a thing because I know it. And I never watch boxing. (Why would anyone do that?) Not even a boxing movie Except maybe that one about Edith Piaf, "La Vie en Rose." Poor Ms. Piaf had a boxer boyfriend! https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0450188/

Great puzzle today…love seeing puzzles from long time constructors…I’m sure I solved the one in 1985 bc I solved every weekday back then. To Steve L and Barry Ancona: thx for the puzzle recommendations…my husband and I solve Connections everyday so looking forward to the puzzle by Wyna Liu. For today, all NYT puzzles solved…wordle in five sadly, one mistake with connections, medium sudoku puzzle easy today so good for beginners…hoping for a more challenging crossword tomorrow. Happy Tuesday!
That was fun!

I loved this puzzle and the theme!  FWIW, I didn't figure out the theme until  I had everything filled in, and even then it took me a few more seconds. Very clever! 

I was not clever about Cincinnati, although the question mark should have clued me in. I spent way too much time looking for a more straightforward definition. Probably because I was born and bred in Cincinnati and wanted to prove something to myself.
I was getting bogged down as I tried to speed solve. Slowed down, I took time to smell the flowers. What a delightful puzzle!  Nice fill and great clues made for a perfect Tuesday.
Fun wordplay and an enjoyable solve, though I thought some clues were odd (especially for a Tuesday). “KAYO” for example.
Fine, fine job Mark. Ingenious, undoubtedly hard to construct, and fun to solve. Don't wait so long for the next one!

A rather amazing puzzle find. A Sunday from February 26, 1995 by Fred Piscop with the title "Every Tom, Dick and Harry." Five 21 letter grid-spanning theme answers and the clue for each and every one of them was: "Tom, Dick and Harry." Those answers: THUMBVANDYKEANDMORGAN SEAVERMARTINANDTRUMAN MIXBUTTONANDBELAFONTE SAWYERCAVETTANDCHAPIN ARNOLDCLARKANDHOUDINI Thought that was just amazing to be able to come up with all of those grid-spanning entries. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=2/26/1995&g=59&d=A ...

To bring those of you who weren't reared in the US up to speed... Count Chocula and Franken Berry cereals debuted in 1971, with Boo Berry joining them in 1973. here's a video of three commercials, one each from the 70s, 80s, and 90s. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4wjgo0cT0x4

Playin’ my cwrth in the cwr, singin’ “and sometimes y and w.

Well 366 in a row today.  Surprised I didn’t give  up during tax season, but, well, if they’ve been easier, kept me in the streak.  

And all the terrific comments here bryng me back. Cwl.
I found this to be a semi-TOUGHENED UP Tuesday, which was a nice semi-challenge.  Got the revealer pretty early on, which helped with the theme entries for sure.
Thanks for the Tuesday fun. I really enjoyed it, and I will admit it took me a little time to see the whole AEIOU thing. Look forward to your next puzzle, no matter what day it pops up.
OCD is not a force of habit for anyone. It’s an anxiety disorder that’s increasingly difficult to diagnose due in part to inaccurate portrayals in media. Sigh.
Only gripe is kayo, its inclusion into any dictionary makes k.o. and ko feel crowded. Especially since TKO doesn’t have a teekayo sister word.

That column photo is worth the price of admission: too cute! (And yet mysterious at first, at least on my tiny phone screen...)

Maybe the photo is an apt analogy for this puzzle, because I came away feeling like the theme was quite subtle. Of course it's spelled out right there in the revealer, but it's approximately the opposite of Sunday's theme, at least in my book!

The resulting rereading of the grid let me see some wonderful connections (amidst the more banal crosswordese). My favorite is the bottom section: After touching upon the arid and often SCORCHing DOHA, we are treated to a flood of watery images, with the IONIAN Sea crossing FROZEN and WATERING TROUGHS, concluding with the NEAP tide to raise all boats. 

Very nice to see Mark Diehl's name on an early-week grid. Congratulations on hitting your Shortz cycle, and hopefully we won't have to wait another 41 years(!) for your next Tuesday NYT publication!
Apropos of absolutely nothing, I just happened to do the following Thursday puzzle from April 2012 and really enjoyed it: https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/daily/2012/04/19

Feel free to correct, but please don’t condescend. 

I admit my baseball knowledge is limited, but I have always thought that a diamond was the same as a plate. It’s just something I grew up knowing.  But apparently that something was wrong. I’ll give you that.

But “ired” still seems less likely than “irked”. Even spellcheck doesn’t like it. As for Apple ICloud,  it may be factually accurate, but I still think it sounds forced. That’s just my opinion, and that’s all I was trying to express.

@Heidi

Yeah, I was going to "like" @Blaise's comment, until that last paragraph. Perhaps it's because I "know" you over many puzzles, but to me your comment didn't read as anything other than your personal opinion. 🤷‍♀️

For a nanosecond during my solve, I considered home instead of BASE, but have never heard of one "bag" being the equivalent of the full diamond. Perhaps it's a regional (Texan?) description? 

As for Apple iCloud, it felt both strained and appropriate — and I have yet to come up with a rationale, lol. As an Android user, the comparable service requires the Google preface, because "Cloud" alone is too broad. But Apple, explicitly, is the owner of the lowercase "i" in their IP. Then again, as @Blaise noted, prefacing with Apple can be necessary when talking with a (much) less technical audience... I think I need more coffee! 😂

@Heidi I've never heard of it called an "Apple iCloud" either. I got "base" because I'm a baseball fan. I mentioned in another comment that it felt more like a Saturday puzzle. This is just one person's opinion, but I thought it crossed the line from "figuring out the answers" to "figuring out how Mark is trying to show that he's clever."

@Heidi
I have loved baseball for 40 years but it's full of technical terms, often used very loosely.
'Diamond' is technically the area within the baselines, which is in fact a diamond in the geometrical sense. It's also a square in the geometrical sense.
More loosely, 'diamond' can refer to the entire area of play within or beyond the foul lines. 
Although the /bases/ are marked with square bags (which too are geometrically  diamonds),  they are /never/ called diamonds or plates. They are called bases or (in slang) sacks.
Home, sometimes called home base or more commonly /home plate/, is not a square or a bag. It is an actual piece of wood, painted white, an irregular pentagon.  It is never called a sack.
So, now to the clue: any runner standing on a base is thereby safe. Every base is situated on the field of play--i.e., the diamond. So, any base is 'a safe place to stand on the diamond.'

That one bothered me too.

As someone on the autism spectrum, I have often seen the attitude that we're choosing to be this way, as though we're indulging in bad behavior. So the notion that OCD is a "habit" like biting your nails rubbed me the wrong way, because it implies a level of control that isn't there. Like you could just stop if you put your mind to it, as you would a nail-biting habit.

It's not a habit; it's wired into our brains.
Habits are brain-wired too. And not necessarily easy to break.
@Jill agreed. Very poor clue that oversimplifies and misidentifies a disorder.

@Jill
I get a bit uneasy when people casually say, “It’s my OCD kicking in.” I had a sister who struggled with OCD, which became intense enough that she required several medications. It’s an extremely debilitating mental health disorder which led to her spending years in a care facility.
I totally understand where you’re coming from. I’m not upset that it popped up in today’s puzzle, but it did bring back some not-so-happy memories for me. I remember, I move on.
Thanks for bringing this to light.

@Nancy J. I have OCD and the clue reminded me of how often people casually say “I’m so OCD about this” or “this is bothering my OCD.” It’s not just repeated actions or “habits” it’s an anxiety disorder. I don’t have a “habit” of quadruple checking that my burners are off…I have an intense fear that if I don’t keep checking until it feels “right” that my house will burn down.

If I reported that I was out with my British friend in London and he had escargots for his entree, my American friends would think he had snails for his main course.  Sure, my British friend would call it an entree, but it still wouldn't make sense to an American audience.

See what I'm getting at?  In Britain, ETON is not a prep school.  But that's what it's considered in the US.  And this is a US paper and a US puzzle.

@Steve L As i have said before in this hallowed arena of cruciverbal debate, this is not a US puzzle and it is not a US paper. It is a New York paper with a global readership, and although I am not American nor even a New Yorker i count myself among its enthusiastic subscribers.  

In this unhappy era i hope that commenters can accept and include a broad spectrum of readers, and not reject the views of anyone with the misfortune to not be a natural-born American. 

I am very happy to accept American spellings and American terminology and God knows American sports and American food brands and American personalities. But not everything is American. I do not have a zip code or an SSN, for example, and my country does not have a President nor a Senate, we do not have Proms and Homecoming or “open carry” (and related ills) or Thanksgiving, and it would be false to claim otherwise just to make things more understandable to Americans.

I remember once a journalist starting a question to Kofi Annan with “As an African-American, do you think…” He got a sharp rebuke.

@Petrol

I think it's impractical to think that the American editors of an American crossword in an American newspaper should be held to the standard that a phrasing has to be  natural-sounding to solvers all over the world.

When you talk about football, I take into consideration that you're European, and immediately realizing you're not talking about the gridiron game.  I consider the source and try to see things through the same lens.  I would say "soccer," to the extent that I would talk about that sport at all, and I know you know the word "soccer," but still realize that it is more natural for you to call it "football," even though "soccer" originated in the UK, just like "football" did.

Calling the sport "soccer," a word your British audience would certainly know, would make any statement you said about it much clearer to all Americans, Canadians, Australians, and Irish people reading your comments.  But since you're more comfortable saying "football," intelligent readers will make the adjustment and understand what you're saying.

Try to do the same for American clues in an American crossword edited by Americans in an American publication.

PS:  I know.  "The English invented the language."

@Jane Wheelaghan
Y is a vowel In this puzzle in 66A and 60D (ENVY, STY).

@Tricia J

I think the linguist was probably referring to the Y in words like play, toy, buy, etc.; in other words, when it’s part of a diphthong. W is similarly a semivowel in similar combinations. 

Y is properly a consonant in most initial-position situations, like yellow or yam; and a true vowel between consonants (myrrh, rhythm) and in final position after a consonant (happy, wry). 

It’s not a semivowel in all circumstances.
@Jane Wheelaghan As you're no doubt finding out, American English is just chuck full of inconsistencies.

@Barry Ancona

I finally broke down and looked it up on Wikipedia:

"In Ancient Greek, the adjective Ionios (Ἰόνιος) was used as an epithet for the sea between Epirus and Italy in which the Ionian Islands are found, because, according to myth, Io swam across it.[2] Although they have the same Latin transliteration and Modern Greek pronunciation, the Ionian Sea and Islands are not related to Ionia, an Anatolian region; in Greek, the Ionian Sea and Ionian Islands are spelled with an omicron (Ιόνια), whereas Ionia has an omega (Ιωνία), reflecting a classical difference in pronunciation."
@The X-Phile apropos Ionia. king Croesus was advised by the oracle at Delphi that if he were to go to war with Persia a great empire would fall. It was his.

@The X-Phile they are named for different people. The islands are named after Ιω who had a rough time after Zeus took a liking to her — long story. The region in Asia Minor is named after Ιων one of patriarchs of the Greeks. 

The ω ο differentiation in the spelling is grammatical in this case.

It's referring to a person's name before marriage to clarify who they are, if they took their husband's name. (I hate the term "maiden name"). Amanda Miller (nee Smith), for example.

Lex,
As a general rule, crossword answers do not have diacritics or punctuation marks.
@Lex, sorry for HeathieJsplaining something you'd already figured out. Not sure what to blame for my apparent inability to read your entire comment before answering. Not usually my MO. ☺️

@Lex, it's from French, the past tense of the verb "naître", to be born. It's the feminine past tense, as it's an "être" verb for conjugation purposes. So, as a woman, I can say, "Je suis née en 1960."  I was born. . . 

In English we typically apply it to women who change their name at marriage. In France, a woman's passport will have her married name, followed by née and the birth name.
